name: Location Services Spying
description: Location Services Spying refers to the theory that governments, corporations, and other entities are secretly tracking individuals' movements and activities through location services enabled on smartphones, computers, and other devices. Proponents claim this surveillance is far more pervasive and intrusive than publicly acknowledged, enabling mass monitoring and potential manipulation. The theory often posits that data collected through location services is used for purposes beyond targeted advertising or improved services, including political monitoring, social control, and even predictive policing. Some believe location data is sold to third parties without users' informed consent, further expanding the scope of potential misuse. Originating in the early 2000s with the rise of GPS-enabled devices and location-based apps, the theory has gained traction with each new revelation of data breaches or government surveillance programs. Evidence cited includes instances of law enforcement obtaining location data without warrants, leaked documents detailing government surveillance capabilities, and research demonstrating the potential for deanonymizing location data even when anonymized. Mainstream perspective acknowledges that location data collection is widespread but argues that it is primarily used for legitimate purposes such as improving navigation, delivering targeted advertising, and providing emergency services. Legal frameworks exist to regulate data collection and protect privacy, although enforcement can be challenging. Critics argue that the fear of pervasive spying is often exaggerated and that the benefits of location-based services outweigh the risks. However, concerns about data security, privacy breaches, and the potential for misuse remain valid and warrant ongoing scrutiny.
subtitle: Secret tracking via location-based technologies

claims.claim: Governments are secretly tracking citizens' movements through location data.
claims.evidence: Leaked documents from intelligence agencies detailing surveillance programs; reports of law enforcement obtaining location data without warrants.
claims.criticism: Governments acknowledge using location data in specific cases with legal oversight, claiming it's necessary for national security and law enforcement. Critics argue the oversight is insufficient.

claims.claim: Corporations are selling user location data to third parties without informed consent.
claims.evidence: Reports of data brokers collecting and selling location data; vague or misleading privacy policies.
claims.criticism: Companies claim to anonymize and aggregate location data before sharing it, complying with privacy regulations. However, research shows anonymization can be easily broken.

claims.claim: Location data is used for predictive policing and social control.
claims.evidence: Use of location data to identify crime hotspots and predict future criminal activity; potential for discriminatory targeting based on location.
claims.criticism: Predictive policing is presented as a tool to allocate resources effectively and prevent crime. Concerns exist about biases in algorithms and potential for reinforcing existing inequalities.

keyFigures.name: Edward Snowden
keyFigures.role: Whistleblower who revealed NSA surveillance programs.

keyFigures.name: Bruce Schneier
keyFigures.role: Security technologist and privacy advocate who has written extensively on surveillance.

mythologyConnections: Argus Panoptes (Greek Mythology): The all-seeing giant with a hundred eyes, representing constant surveillance.
The Eye of Providence: A symbol associated with secret societies and the idea of an all-seeing power.

timeline.event: Rise of GPS-enabled smartphones and location-based services.
Edward Snowden leaks reveal NSA surveillance programs, including location data collection.
Cambridge Analytica scandal raises concerns about data privacy and misuse.

sources.author: Ron Deibert
sources.work: Black Code: Surveillance, Privacy, and the Dark Side of the Internet
sources.title: Ron Deibert

sources.author: Byron Tau
sources.work: Means of Control: How the Hidden World of Pay-to-Play Lobbying Shaped American Democracy
sources.title: Byron Tau
